original_abstract:
Given the rising interest in alternative medicine, dietary medicinal plants are being extensively studied for their therapeutic potential. This study focused on isolating and characterizing polyphenol complexes from eight indigenous plants—*Mentha longifolia, M. arvensis, Tinospora cordifolia, Cymbopogon citratus, Foeniculum vulgare, Cassia absus, Camellia sinensis*, and *Trachyspermum ammi*—and assessing their antioxidant, antimicrobial, antidiabetic, and cytotoxic activities.

Phytochemical screening confirmed that all extracts were rich in alkaloids, phenols, and flavonoids. UHPLC analysis identified 25 specific polyphenolic compounds, including phenolic acids and flavonoids. *Camellia sinensis* exhibited the highest abundance of phenolics (20–116% higher than other species), while 3-Feroylquinic acid was identified as a major constituent across several plants, including *M. longifolia* and *C. absus*. Other significant compounds, such as rosmarinic, chlorogenic, and gallic acids, as well as rutin and quercetin, were quantified in substantial concentrations.

The biological assays revealed significant functional potential across the samples. All medicinal plant polyphenolic extracts (MPPE) demonstrated robust antioxidant activity in DPPH, hydroxyl, nitric oxide, and superoxide radical scavenging assays, correlating with their high total phenolic and flavonoid contents. In terms of enzyme inhibition, *M. arvensis, C. sinensis*, and *F. vulgare* showed potent antidiabetic activity through the inhibition of $\alpha$-glucosidase and $\alpha$-amylase. Cytotoxicity tests (MTT assay) against Caco-2 colon cancer cells highlighted *T. ammi* and *C. citratus* as having the strongest antitumor potential. Furthermore, *F. vulgare* and *C. sinensis* displayed marked antibacterial efficacy against common pathogens, including *S. aureus, B. cereus, E. coli*, and *S. enterica*.

Principal component analysis (PCA) effectively categorized the plants based on these functional traits. These results validate the traditional use of these indigenous species and underscore their value as natural reservoirs of bioactive compounds. This study highlights the untapped potential of these plants for development into functional foods and pharmaceutical agents.
humanized_abstract:
As far as possible interest in alternative medicines has enhanced the magnitude of research on medicinal plants as dietary adjuncts. The present investigation was aimed at isolation and characterization of polyphenol complexes from eight native plant species; *Mentha longifolia* (EMF), *Mentha arvensis* (EF), *Tinospora cordifolia* (TF), *Cymbopogon citratus* (GC), *Foeniculum vulgare* (FX), *Cassia absus* (CAB) and *Camellia sinensis* (LC) and *Trachyspermum ammi* (CAF) alongwith the evaluation of their antioxidant, antimicrobial, antidiabetic as well as cytotoxic activities.

Phytochemical screening revealed that all plant extracts were enriched in alkaloids, phenols and flavonoids. Using UHPLC analysis, 25 individual polyphenols; phenolic acids and flavonoids were identified. Camellia sinensis revealed the enrichment of the highest content of phenolics compared to other plants with an increase of 20-116% with 3-Feroylquinic acid being a common polyphenol identified in all plant species and in large quantities in Matricaria longifolia and C. absus. Other plant extracts had significant quantities of rosmarinic, chlorogenic and gallic acids and rutin and quercetin.

Biological assays revealed rich biological functions in most of the samples. All medicinal plant polyphenolic extracts (MPPE) displayed strong free radical scavenging activities through DPPH, hydroxyl, nitric oxide and superoxide radicals scavenging assays due to their high contents of total phenolics and flavonoids. More interestingly, extracts of M. arvensis, C. sinensis and F. vulgare have been proven to exert promising antidiabetic activities through the inhibition of $\alpha$-glucosidase and $\alpha$-amylase enzymes. In MTT assay on Caco-2 colon cancer cells, extracts of T. ammi, C. citratus showed strong cancer inhibitory activities on colon cancer cells. Extracts of F. vulgare and C. sinensis have shown impressive antibacterial activities towards various bacteria strains, such as Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus cereus, Escherichia coli and Salmonella enterica.

Multivariate analysis, Principal component analysis (PCA), was employed to study the plants on the basis of functional characteristics. The scientific authentication of traditional uses, and the role of these plant species as treasure house of bioactive molecules points out a vast scope in utilizing them as rich source of functional foods, biomedicines etc.

This study focused on isolating and characterizing polyphenol complexes from eight indigenous plants—*Mentha longifolia, M. arvensis, Tinospora cordifolia, Cymbopogon citratus, Foeniculum vulgare, Cassia absus, Camellia sinensis*, and *Trachyspermum ammi*—and assessing their antioxidant, antimicrobial, antidiabetic, and cytotoxic activities.
